burial.plot_notes: Sparkman Hillcrest Memorial Park, Dallas TX

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1913 son of workmen's compensation reformer
narrative_blocks.body: Eldest son of Talcott Huntington Russell (Yale 1869 + Columbia Law 1872 + Russell Process Company secretary-treasurer + first chairman of CT Workmen's Compensation Commission). Yale BA 1913. Moved to Dallas Texas where he died in 1971 at 79 — buried Sparkman Hillcrest Memorial Park.

burial.plot_notes: Sparkman Hillcrest Memorial Park, Dallas TX

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1913 son of workmen's compensation reformer
narrative_blocks.body: Eldest son of Talcott Huntington Russell (Yale 1869 + Columbia Law 1872 + Russell Process Company secretary-treasurer + first chairman of CT Workmen's Compensation Commission). Yale BA 1913. Moved to Dallas Texas where he died in 1971 at 79 — buried Sparkman Hillcrest Memorial Park.

burial.plot_notes: Sparkman Hillcrest Memorial Park, Dallas TX (alongside husband Philip)

narrative_blocks.header: Texas wife of a Yale lawyer's son
narrative_blocks.body: Born Texas 1903; married Philip Gray Russell (Yale 1913, son of Talcott Huntington Russell). Survived her husband by 21 years; both buried at Sparkman Hillcrest Memorial Park in Dallas — the Texas branch of the Russell-Talcott-Hooker line.

sources.label: Yale University Obituary Record — Talcott Huntington Russell (B.A. 1869)
Yale Library Manuscripts MS 1213 — Russell Process Company Records
Find a Grave — Talcott Huntington Russell

cross_connections.display_label: Carried the Talcott name third generation, after grandfather and aunt.

notable.primary_url_label: Yale Library MS 1213 Russell Process Company Records

institutions.notes: Brother Edward H. Russell the Russell Process inventor was general manager.

narrative_blocks.header: Donated his ancestor's 1638 sea chest to history
narrative_blocks.body: In 1888 his family donated the William Russell sea chest, dating to 1638, to the New Haven Colony Historical Society. The provenance letter named him as the living end of a 250-year chain.

narrative_blocks.header: Father co-founded Skull and Bones at Yale
narrative_blocks.body: His father William Huntington Russell co-founded the secret society at Yale in 1832. The family's Yale connections ran through both the Russell and the Talcott lines for generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Third generation to carry the Talcott name
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Matthew Talcott Russell had been named for the childless Col. Matthew Talcott who funded his education. He was the third in his line to carry the Talcott name forward.

narrative_blocks.header: First chairman of CT workmen's compensation 1913
narrative_blocks.body: In 1911 he was retained by the CT legislative committee to draft workmen's compensation legislation; his draft 'formed the framework of much of the legislation finally adopted.' When Connecticut adopted the system in 1913 he was made first chairman of the commission — resigning 1.5 years later due to ill health.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Law instructor on municipal corporations
narrative_blocks.body: From 1892 until 1900 he served as instructor on municipal corporations at the Yale School of Law — an 8-year teaching tenure during his New Haven private-practice years. The position followed his New Haven Board of Councilmen presidency in the city's municipal-government era.

narrative_blocks.header: Secretary-treasurer of the Russell Process Company
narrative_blocks.body: Together with his brother Edward — inventor of the Russell Process for silver extraction from low-grade ores — he ran the New York-headquartered Russell Process Company that licensed metallurgy patents through the 1880s-1890s western US silver mining boom. Much of the company's business was conducted out of his New Haven law office.

narrative_blocks.header: Conference on Uniform State Laws founding treasurer
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of the first members + treasurer of the Conference on Uniform State Laws (now the Uniform Law Commission) — the body that drafts model legislation for adoption by US states. He chaired the Commercial Law Committee, shaping early uniform commercial law jurisprudence.

Prepared at his father's school (General Russell's Collegiate and Commercial Institute) and at Lawrence Academy in Groton MA. After Yale BA 1869 he studied 1 year at Yale Law before transferring to Columbia Law School, earning his LL.B. 1872 — that year was admitted to the Connecticut bar.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

education.notes: Groton MA (Sam-Sr's school + his own preparatory)
Studied 1 year at Yale Law before transferring to Columbia.

career.location: New Haven CT office (NYC company HQ)
career.notes: Brother Edward H. Russell was general manager + inventor; C.A. Stetefeldt was president.
First chairman when CT adopted workmen's compensation 1913; resigned after 1.5 years due to ill health.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT (alongside husband Talcott)

parents.father_name: Captain William W. Low, U.S. Navy

narrative_blocks.header: Navy captain's daughter who married a Yale lawyer
narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of Captain William W. Low, U.S. Navy, and Evelina (Peck) Low. Married Talcott Huntington Russell — Russell Process Company secretary-treasurer + workmen's compensation reformer — at New Haven on 10 December 1889.

cross_connections.display_label: Second cousin who founded America's largest China opium trading house.
Co-founded Skull and Bones together at Yale in 1832.
Co-founder's son who became 27th US President.
Co-founder's son and Bonesman like his father.
Co-founder's son who founded Taft School.
Hooker descendant who joined the society sixty years later.
Joined the society he co-founded 78 years later.
His Pierpont ancestor founded Yale; he founded Skull and Bones.

notable.notable_blurb: Co-founded Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft, 1832

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft
narrative_blocks.body: In 1832 at Yale, he co-founded Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft — both Yale class of 1833, both Hooker descendants. The senior secret society became the most influential of its kind in American history, with members later including three Presidents, four Supreme Court justices, and numerous senators and cabinet members.

narrative_blocks.header: Second cousin to America's biggest opium trader
narrative_blocks.body: His second cousin Samuel Wadsworth Russell was the largest American opium trading house operator in China through the 1820s-30s. The Russell family fortune that helped support Skull and Bones's early decades came partly from Russell & Company's Canton operations.

In 1888 he donated his ancestor William Russell's sea chest to the New Haven Colony Historical Society — preserving a tangible 18th-century Russell-family artifact. The chest later passed through the family with George Huntington Larned loaning it back for further display.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of ten including Talcott Huntington Russell
narrative_blocks.body: He married Mary Elizabeth Hubbard on 29 August 1836 at Clinton NY; they had ten children including Talcott Huntington Russell. His son carried the Talcott + Russell + Huntington surname chain — three founding-era Connecticut family names compressed into one given-name string.

education.notes: — Yale 1833. Co-founded Skull and Bones 1832 with Alphonso Taft. Son of Matthew Talcott Russell (H00856). Second cousin of Samuel Wadsworth Russell (China trader).

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
